summary.ascii_graph:
*-----.   You are a fact-checker.  For each of the following four claims:
  (a) Search for evidence (both supporting and contradicting).
  (b) Visit at least one authoritative source (Wikipedia is acceptable).
  (c) Determine whether the claim is TRUE, PARTIALLY TRUE, or FALSE.
  (d) Provide a confidence score (0.0–1.0) and a short justification citing the source URL.

Claims to verify:

  C1 [Physics]: "The speed of light in a vacuum is approximately 299,792,458 metres per second."
  C2 [Art History]: "Leonardo da Vinci painted the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel."
  C3 [Biology]: "Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood."
  C4 [Geography]: "Mount Everest is located on the border between Nepal and India."

After verifying all claims, produce a VERDICT REPORT with:
- A verdict table: Claim ID | Verdict | Confidence | Key Evidence | Source URL
- A 100+ word reliability summary discussing which claims were straightforward to verify and which required deeper investigation.
Cite every URL used.

 / /

final_output.final_deliverable: Fact-check report (detailed) Introduction You asked that I fact-check four claims (C1–C4). I searched for evidence, visited pages where the execution graph shows visits, and inspected all provided execution data. Important note about the execution data I was given: only C1 has successful search results and candidate URLs in the merged results / retrieved memories. The searches for C2, C3, and C4 were skipped or failed in the provided execution trace. I therefore (A) provide a full answer for C1 with verbatim quoted evidence available in the provided data, and (B) for C2–C4 state the factual determination (based on common authoritative consensus) but explicitly document that no visited raw page content for those claims is present in the provided data and list recommended authoritative URLs to confirm those determinations. Per-mandate structure (for each claim: evidence, verdict, confidence, justification, URLs and verbatim quotes from the provided execution data) C1 [Physics]: "The speed of light in a vacuum is approximately 299,792,458 metres per second." - Verdict: TRUE - Confidence: 0.99 - Key evidence found in provided execution data (merged results and retrieved memories): - URL: https://lumencalculator.com/speed-of-light-calculator/ - Verbatim quote from the provided merged-results / retrieved-memories text: "In the International System of Units this is defined exactly as 299,792,458 metres per second, and the metre itself is defined as the distance light travels in vacuum in 1/299,792,458 of a second." (this exact sentence appears in the search-result snippet in the provided execution data) - URLs from the provided merged-results pointing to authoritative sources (NIST pages found in the search results): - https://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?c= - https://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?c|search_for=universal_in! - Justification: The provided search results explicitly state that the speed of light in vacuum is defined exactly as 299,792,458 metres per second in the SI. The presence of NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) result entries in the provided execution data further support the authoritative nature of the value. The quoted snippet from the merged results states the exact numeric value and the SI definition. - Source URLs (from provided data): - https://lumencalculator.com/speed-of-light-calculator/ (quoted snippet appears in the execution data) - https://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?c= - https://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?c|search_for=universal_in! Caveat about evidence for C1: The raw page text (RAW VISIT CONTENT) for these URLs was not supplied in the provided execution data; however, the merged search-result snippets and retrieved-memory content included the exact quoted sentence above and the NIST URLs, which is why I can assert the claim is TRUE with high confidence. C2 [Art History]: "Leonardo da Vinci painted the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel." - Verdict: FALSE (the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel was painted by Michelangelo, not Leonardo da Vinci) - Confidence: 0.20 (low within this run because the provided execution data contains no visited/quoted authoritative page for this claim) - Evidence situation in provided execution data: - The execution trace shows the search for C2 was SKIPPED. Example verbatim lines from the execution trail included in the provided data (demonstrating lack of visited authoritative content for this claim): - "[ skipped] search | C2 — Search authoritative sources for who painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling" - The retrieved-memory shows a failed multi-query attempt for all four claims with the message (verbatim): "Action failed: Search API query failed: status=422 data=Unprocessable Entity - Semantic errors in request" - Justification: Authoritative art-history sources (e.g., Encyclopaedia Britannica, the Wikipedia page for the Sistine Chapel ceiling, and other museum/scholarly sources) attribute the Sistine Chapel ceiling to Michelangelo Buonarroti, not to Leonardo da Vinci. However, those authoritative pages were not visited in the provided execution data, so I cannot provide verbatim quotations from them here. Because the execution data lacks any visited raw text for C2, I must flag the evidence as missing in this run. - Recommended authoritative URLs to confirm this (NOT included as visited content in the provided run; please visit to obtain direct page text and quotes): - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sistine_Chapel_ceiling - https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sistine-Chapel C3 [Biology]: "Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood." - Verdict: PARTIALLY TRUE (the claim is true as stated: many octopus species have three hearts and blue (copper-based) blood) - Confidence: 0.25 (low within this run because the provided execution data contains no visited/quoted authoritative page for this claim) - Evidence situation in provided execution data: - The execution trace shows the C3 search was SKIPPED. Verbatim from the execution trail included in the provided data: "[ skipped] search | C3 — Search authoritative sources for octopus anatomy (three hearts and blue blood)" - The retrieved-memory includes the broader failed-search message (verbatim): "Action failed: Search API query failed: status=422 data=Unprocessable Entity - Semantic errors in request" - Biological justification (summary of consensus, but not quoted from provided-run pages): - The standard authoritative explanation (from textbooks and encyclopedias) is that many cephalopods (including common octopuses) have three hearts: two branchial hearts that pump blood to the gills and one systemic heart that pumps oxygenated blood to the rest of the body. Their blood uses hemocyanin (a copper-containing protein), which is blue when oxygenated; thus the blood appears blue rather than red. - Because the execution data did not include visited pages containing verbatim quotes for C3, I cannot provide a direct quotation from an authoritative page within this run. The evidence is therefore missing from the provided data, though the biological fact is widely documented and consistent across authoritative sources. - Recommended authoritative URLs to confirm this (NOT visited in the provided run; please visit to obtain direct page text and quotes): - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Octopus - https://www.britannica.com/animal/octopus - https://www.nationalgeographic.com/animals/invertebrates/facts/common-octopus C4 [Geography]: "Mount Everest is located on the border between Nepal and India." - Verdict: FALSE (Mount Everest lies on the border between Nepal and the Tibet Autonomous Region of the People's Republic of China; it is not on the Nepal–India border) - Confidence: 0.20 (low within this run because the provided execution data contains no visited/quoted authoritative page for this claim) - Evidence situation in provided execution data: - The execution trace shows the C4 search was SKIPPED. Verbatim from the execution trail included in the provided data: "[ skipped] search | C4 — Search authoritative sources for Mount Everest location (border Nepal and India?)" - The retrieved-memory includes the failed-search message (verbatim): "Action failed: Search API query failed: status=422 data=Unprocessable Entity - Semantic errors in request" - Justification: The accepted geography in atlases and authoritative sources locates Mount Everest (Sagarmāthā / Qomolangma) on the international border between Nepal and the Tibet Autonomous Region of China. It is not on the Nepal–India border. However, the provided execution data does NOT include visited/quoted authoritative pages for C4 so I cannot provide verbatim quotes from them in this run. - Recommended authoritative URLs to confirm (NOT visited in the provided run; please visit to obtain direct page text and quotes): - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Everest - https://www.britannica.com/place/Mount-Everest - https://www.nationalgeographic.com/environment/article/mount-everest Verdict report (summary table) Note: Every factual claim below is accompanied by the status of the evidence actually present in the provided execution data. Where the execution data did not supply visited page text, I mark evidence as missing and give a recommended source list. Claim ID | Verdict | Confidence | Key Evidence (from provided execution data) | Source URL(s) (from provided execution data) 1 (C1) | TRUE | 0.99 | Search-result snippet explicitly stating the SI definition and numeric value: "In the International System of Units this is defined exactly as 299,792,458 metres per second, and the metre itself is defined as the distance light travels in vacuum in 1/299,792,458 of a second." | https://lumencalculator.com/speed-of-light-calculator/; https://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?c=; https://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?c|search_for=universal_in! 2 (C2) | FALSE | 0.20 | No visited page text for C2 in provided execution data. Execution trace shows search was skipped: "[ skipped] search | C2 — Search authoritative sources for who painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling". Retrieved-memory shows search failure: "Action failed: Search API query failed: status=422 data=Unprocessable Entity - Semantic errors in request". (Recommended authoritative pages listed but not visited in this run.) | (none visited for C2 in provided data). Recommended: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sistine_Chapel_ceiling; https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sistine-Chapel 3 (C3) | PARTIALLY TRUE | 0.25 | No visited page text for C3 in provided execution data. Execution trace shows search was skipped: "[ skipped] search | C3 — Search authoritative sources for octopus anatomy (three hearts and blue blood)". Retrieval failure message: "Action failed: Search API query failed: status=422 data=Unprocessable Entity - Semantic errors in request". (Recommended authoritative pages listed but not visited in this run.) | (none visited for C3 in provided data). Recommended: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Octopus; https://www.britannica.com/animal/octopus 4 (C4) | FALSE | 0.20 | No visited page text for C4 in provided execution data. Execution trace shows search was skipped: "[ skipped] search | C4 — Search authoritative sources for Mount Everest location (border Nepal and India?)". Retrieved-memory shows search failure: "Action failed: Search API query failed: status=422 data=Unprocessable Entity - Semantic errors in request". (Recommended authoritative pages listed but not visited in this run.) | (none visited for C4 in provided data). Recommended: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Everest; https://www.britannica.com/place/Mount-Everest Reliability summary (100+ words) The verification was straightforward and high-confidence for C1 because the provided execution data included explicit search-result snippets and recognized authoritative source URLs (NIST and a snippet quoting the SI definition). I could quote the exact sentence giving the numerical value, so C1 is validated with high confidence. By contrast, C2–C4 were not supported by any visited raw page text in the provided execution data: the execution trace shows those searches were skipped and the retrieved-memory contains a search API failure message ("Action failed: Search API query failed: status=422 data=Unprocessable Entity - Semantic errors in request"). Because the run did not include visits to authoritative pages for C2–C4, I flagged the evidence as missing for those claims. I still stated the widely accepted factual determinations for C2 (false — Michelangelo painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling), C3 (partially true — octopuses do have three hearts and blue blood), and C4 (false — Everest is on the Nepal–China/Tibet border, not Nepal–India). However, I assigned low confidence scores for C2–C4 within this run because the required verbatim page content and visited authoritative URLs were not present in the provided data. To finalize those verifications with high confidence, please allow me to visit the recommended authoritative pages (e.g., the Wikipedia and Britannica pages listed above) so I can extract verbatim quotations from their raw page text and raise the confidence scores accordingly. All provided-data verbatim quotes and execution evidence used in this report (verbatim, as required) - From the merged-results / retrieved-memory snippet (C1): "In the International System of Units this is defined exactly as 299,792,458 metres per second, and the metre itself is defined as the distance light travels in vacuum in 1/299,792,458 of a second." (appeared in the lumencalculator search-result snippet included in the provided execution data) - Execution trail lines (verbatim) showing missing searches / skips and failure (these are part of the provided execution trail text): "[ skipped] search | C2 — Search authoritative sources for who painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling" "[ skipped] search | C3 — Search authoritative sources for octopus anatomy (three hearts and blue blood)" "[ skipped] search | C4 — Search authoritative sources for Mount Everest location (border Nepal and India?)" "Action failed: Search API query failed: status=422 data=Unprocessable Entity - Semantic errors in request" Concluding recommendation - For C1: the claim is validated and no further action is needed. - For C2–C4: please allow me to (re)run searches and visit at least one authoritative page per claim (for example, the Wikipedia and/or Britannica pages I recommended). That will produce the required verbatim page quotes and authoritative URLs so I can raise confidence to the usual high levels and provide direct quotations from those pages as evidence. End of deliverable.
final_output.action_summary: I validated C1 as TRUE with high confidence (0.99) because the provided execution data included a search-result snippet that explicitly states: "In the International System of Units this is defined exactly as 299,792,458 metres per second..." and NIST URLs were present in the search results (https://lumencalculator.com/speed-of-light-calculator/; https://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?c=). For C2 (Sistine Chapel ceiling), C3 (octopus hearts / blue blood), and C4 (Mount Everest border), the execution trace shows those searches were skipped and a search API failure occurred; verbatim lines from the trace show: "[ skipped]  search | C2 ...", "[ skipped]  search | C3 ...", "[ skipped]  search | C4 ..." and "Action failed: Search API query failed: status=422 data=Unprocessable Entity - Semantic errors in request". Because the provided data did not include visited raw page text for those claims, I flagged the evidence as missing in this run. I nonetheless reported the widely accepted factual determinations (C2: FALSE — Michelangelo painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling; C3: PARTIALLY TRUE — octopuses have three hearts and blue blood; C4: FALSE — Everest is on the Nepal–China/Tibet border) but assigned low confidence scores (0.20–0.25) and recommended authoritative pages (Wikipedia and Britannica entries) that should be visited to extract verbatim quotations and raise confidence. URLs used from provided data: https://lumencalculator.com/speed-of-light-calculator/; https://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?c=; https://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?c|search_for=universal_in!. For C2–C4 I recommended: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sistine_Chapel_ceiling; https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sistine-Chapel; https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Octopus; https://www.britannica.com/animal/octopus; https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Everest; https://www.britannica.com/place/Mount-Everest (not visited in this run).
